title: Live iTerm2 apply has no timeout; a hung websocket blocks the watch daemon forever
description: apply_preset_to_current_session() calls iterm2.run_until_complete(main) directly with no timeout. The iterm2 library awaits the coroutine with no read/ping timeout (see .venv/.../iterm2/connection.py:430 `return await coro(self)` and the connect coro uses close_timeout=0), so if iTerm2 accepts the websocket but never responds to async_get_app / async_set_profile_properties (iTerm2 main thread wedged, a modal/AppleScript dialog blocking, or the daemon half-dead), run_until_complete blocks indefinitely. This is exactly the failure the authors anticipated and defended against in iterm_connection.py:28-30 ("run_until_complete drives its own event loop with no timeout, so an unresponsive daemon would block the CLI forever. Run it on a daemon thread and bound the wait"), but that protection was NOT applied here. The unattended autolaunch daemon calls this on every mode switch via run_watch_live -> apply_preset (watch_live.py:177), which only catches RuntimeError; a hang never raises, so the entire ~10-year background watch loop (watch_daemon.py:126 duration=315360000) freezes, holds the connection, and stops adapting with no log line. Trigger: start watch-live --yes against an iTerm2 whose API thread is blocked by a confirmation dialog, then change the background to force a switch.
fix: Mirror the iterm_connection.py pattern: run iterm2.run_until_complete(main) on a daemon thread, join with a bounded timeout (e.g. 15s), and on timeout return an applied=False LiveApplyResult (or raise RuntimeError, which the watch loop already handles) instead of blocking. Factor the threaded-bounded-run helper out of iterm_connection.py and reuse it in live_iterm.py.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the actual code and git history.

BUG EXISTS (live_iterm.py:61-62): apply_preset_to_current_session calls `iterm2.run_until_complete(main)` directly and synchronously with no timeout. run_until_complete drives its own event loop and blocks until the coroutine finishes; main awaits async_get_app and async_set_profile_properties, which await websocket responses with no read/ping bound. If iTerm2 accepts the connection but its API thread is wedged (modal/AppleScript dialog, half-dead daemon), the call blocks indefinitely.

AUTHORS DEFENDED AGAINST THIS ELSEWHERE BUT NOT HERE: iterm_connection.py:28-30 carries the exact comment the claim quotes ("run_until_complete drives its own event loop with no timeout, so an unresponsive daemon would block the CLI forever. Run it on a daemon thread and bound the wait"), and lines 33-51 implement that protection (daemon Thread + worker.join(timeout) + is_alive() check returning a failure result). live_iterm.py has no equivalent.

CALL CHAIN TO UNATTENDED DAEMON CONFIRMED: watch_daemon.py:126 launches `watch-live --yes --duration 315360000` (~10 years). run_watch_live (watch_live.py:131,177) calls apply_preset(mode) = apply_preset_to_current_session on each non-dry-run switch, wrapped only in `except RuntimeError` (line 178). A hang never raises, so the except never fires and the loop at lines 146-204 freezes silently with no log line.

GENUINELY NEW, NOT A ROUND-1 DUPLICATE: `git log` shows the timeout fix commit c37328e ("fix: bound iTerm2 connection probe with a timeout") modified ONLY iterm_connection.py. live_iterm.py's sole prior change (5b6a3f9 audit cleanup) was a `_setter_mappings`->`setter_mappings` rename (verified via git show) and did not touch the timeout. The apply-path gap was never closed.

SEVERITY: HIGH is appropriate. The trigger is a real but somewhat narrow operational condition (iTerm2 accepts the websocket then stops responding), and the impact is a silently-frozen background watcher (liveness/availability loss), not data loss or a security flaw. That is a genuine HIGH, not CRITICAL.

Relevant files: src/term_chameleon/live_iterm.py (lines 46-66), src/term_chameleon/iterm_connection.py (lines 28-51), src/term_chameleon/watch_live.py (lines 123-184), src/term_chameleon/watch_daemon.py (lines 103-142). Proposed fix (factor out a threaded-bounded-run helper from iterm_connection.py and reuse it in live_iterm.py, returning applied=False or raising RuntimeError on timeout) is sound and the watch loop already handles RuntimeError.

title: iterm2 event loop is never closed; repeated mode switches leak fds in the long-running watch daemon
description: Each call to apply_preset_to_current_session() invokes the module-level iterm2.run_until_complete, which constructs a fresh Connection() (.venv/.../iterm2/connection.py:517) whose Connection.run() does `loop = asyncio.new_event_loop(); asyncio.set_event_loop(loop); ... loop.run_until_complete(...)` and NEVER closes the loop (connection.py:225-246, return at :246 with no loop.close()). The only close path (`if self.loop is not None: self.loop.close()` at :222) helps only when the SAME Connection instance is reused, but a new Connection is created on every call, so it never fires. Each unclosed event loop retains its selector (epoll/kqueue) plus self-pipe socketpair fds. Measured empirically with this venv: 50 unclosed new_event_loop()+run_until_complete() calls leaked ~81 fds. In the watch daemon (designed to run for years, watch_daemon.py:126), every mode switch leaks fds; on macOS with the default soft limit of 256 open files the process will eventually die with 'Too many open files', leaving a stale pidfile and a dead autoswitcher.
fix: Run each iterm2 apply in a short-lived subprocess (clean fd reclamation on exit), or wrap the call so the event loop is explicitly closed afterward (e.g. drive the coroutine yourself: create loop, run, finally loop.close()). Combined with the timeout fix above, a per-apply subprocess gives both bounded time and guaranteed fd reclamation. At minimum, periodically gc.collect() will not reliably reclaim because set_event_loop holds the last loop; explicit close is required.

verdict.reasoning:
VERIFIED TRUE and NEW.

Code-path confirmation (all line references checked against real files):
1. live_iterm.py:62 — apply_preset_to_current_session() calls module-level iterm2.run_until_complete(main). It has no event-loop cleanup, gc, or subprocess wrapper (grep confirmed: no close/gc/subprocess in the file).
2. connection.py:500-517 — module-level run_until_complete() constructs a FRESH Connection() on every call and delegates to Connection.run_until_complete -> run().
3. connection.py:146 — Connection.__init__ sets self.loop = None.
4. connection.py:210-252 — run() does `if self.loop is not None: self.loop.close()` (222-223). Since a brand-new Connection always has self.loop=None, this close path never fires. Then `loop = asyncio.new_event_loop(); asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)` (225-226), `self.loop = loop` (245), `loop.run_until_complete(...)` (246), and returns at 252 with NO loop.close(). Exactly as claimed.
5. Caller chain confirmed: watch_live.py:146 `while True` loop calls apply_preset(mode) at line 177 on every switch; default apply_preset = apply_preset_to_current_session (line 127). watch_daemon.py generates a command with --duration 315360000 (10 years), confirming the long-running daemon designed to run indefinitely.

Empirical reproduction in the project's own .venv (python3.11):
- 50 iterations of new_event_loop()+set_event_loop()+run_until_complete() WITHOUT close() leaked 81 fds (base 4 -> 85). This matches the claim's "~81 fds" exactly.
- Same loop WITH explicit loop.close() leaked 0 fds, confirming both the root cause (unclosed loop retaining selector + self-pipe socketpair fds) and that the proposed fix works.

NEW vs round-1 (27 fixed issues): git history shows the round-1 work was af8a318 (26 audit findings) + c37328e (bound iTerm2 connection PROBE with a timeout). The probe fix touched iterm_connection.py and addressed the no-timeout blocking concern via a daemon thread — but that thread path ALSO never closes the loop, and live_iterm.py's apply path was never given any loop-lifecycle or fd-cleanup treatment. `git log -S 'loop.close'` returns nothing across all history. a23325a ("keep live watcher running after apply errors") only changed error handling in watch_live.py and actually increases leak exposure over the daemon lifetime. So the fd-leak in the long-running apply path is genuinely new and unaddressed.

Severity assessment: On macOS the default soft RLIMIT_NOFILE is 256. Each mode switch leaks roughly 1-2 fds (selector + socketpair). A daemon designed to run for years will, after a few hundred mode switches, hit EMFILE 'Too many open files', after which the process dies leaving a stale pidfile (watch_daemon autolaunch checks pid liveness, so a stale-but-not-running pid would prevent restart only if the pid is reused; more importantly the autoswitcher is simply dead). This is a real resource-exhaustion bug causing eventual daemon death, but it requires many mode switches over a long uptime to manifest, and cooldown (default 10s) plus stable-sample gating throttles switch frequency. It degrades a background convenience feature rather than corrupting data or exposing security. HIGH is the right severity (genuine bug that kills the long-running daemon), though it sits at the lower edge of HIGH given the time-to-manifest. I keep it at HIGH as claimed.

Minor note: macOS uses kqueue selector (KqueueSelector) whose fd count per loop may differ slightly from the Linux epoll repro above, but the leak mechanism (unclosed loop + self-pipe socketpair) is platform-independent; the repro demonstrates the leak unambiguously.

title: e2e-stage returns exit 0 even when its embedded visual-contrast checks fail (masks failures that visual-test reports as exit 1)
description: `_e2e_stage` always prints '[ok] e2e staging bundle passed' and returns 0. It calls run_e2e_stage which at e2e_stage.py:38 does `visual_json, visual_md, _checks = write_visual_report(...)` and discards `_checks` entirely — the same visual report that the standalone `visual-test` command uses to return exit 1 on failed contrast. Verified counterexample: for a profile with background #FFFFFF / foreground #F2F2F2 (contrast 1.12:1), `term-chameleon visual-test <profile>` exits 1 (prints multiple [fail] lines), but `term-chameleon e2e-stage <profile>` on the identical profile prints '[ok] e2e staging bundle passed' and exits 0. A user running e2e-stage as a gate gets a false green. Additionally, when --capture is requested but the screenshot fails (screenshot_captured False/None), e2e-stage still returns 0, whereas the standalone screenshot-test command returns 1 in that case (cli.py:1234-1235).
fix: Have run_e2e_stage propagate the visual checks' pass/fail (and screenshot capture status when --capture is set) into the report, and make _e2e_stage return 1 when any embedded visual check fails or a requested capture fails — matching the exit semantics of visual-test and screenshot-test.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading the cited code. e2e_stage.py:38 does `visual_json, visual_md, _checks = write_visual_report(...)` and discards `_checks`; E2EStageReport carries no pass/fail field for visual checks. cli.py:1310-1320 (_e2e_stage) unconditionally prints '[ok] e2e staging bundle passed' and `return 0` with no branch inspecting any failure. By contrast, _visual_test (cli.py:906-916) computes `failed = [c for c in checks if not c.passed]` and `return 1` when any check fails (VisualCheck.passed is `contrast >= threshold`, visual.py:22-23). So a profile with low contrast (e.g. bg #FFFFFF / fg #F2F2F2, ~1.12:1) makes `visual-test` exit 1 while `e2e-stage` on the same profile exits 0 — a genuine false-green. The secondary claim also holds: _screenshot_test returns 1 when `--capture` is set but `not report.screenshot.captured` (cli.py:1234-1235), whereas _e2e_stage records screenshot_captured (e2e_stage.py:54-56) but never tests it for the exit code, so a failed requested capture still yields exit 0. Newness: the discard was introduced in the original e2e-stage commit (2ef4dc3) and never modified since (verified via git log -S); it is present at current HEAD and is a distinct exit-semantics divergence, not a restatement of a round-1 fix. Severity: HIGH is appropriate — a CI gate using e2e-stage gets a false pass on contrast/capture failures; not CRITICAL since no data-loss/security and the standalone visual-test/screenshot-test commands still exit correctly.

title: Otsu threshold returns the dark-class bin center, not the inter-class boundary; inverts glyph/background classification for dark-text-on-light images
line: 93-101 (best_t = t_i at line 98-100; t_i defined line 93)
description:
_otsu_threshold sets best_t to t_i, the *bin center of the last bin assigned to class 0*, rather than the boundary/valley between the two classes. Because the optimal Otsu partition for bimodal luminance data places the entire dark cluster in class 0 and the entire light cluster in class 1, best_t ends up equal to the dark cluster's luminance, NOT the valley between clusters.

Numeric counterexamples (all verified by running the code):
- Symmetric bimodal [0.2]*100 + [0.8]*100 -> _otsu_threshold returns 0.2012 (valley should be ~0.5).
- Pure [0.0]*50 + [1.0]*50 -> returns 0.00195 (should be ~0.5).
- Realistic dark-text-on-light [0.9]*900 + [0.02]*100 -> returns 0.0217 (should be ~0.46).

This matters because estimate_raster_text_contrast (line 187-191) builds split_fn as `abs(lum - adaptive_t) >= glyph_delta`. When adaptive_t sits on the dark cluster, the *background* (light) pixels become 'far' from the threshold and are classified as glyphs, while the actual dark text pixels are classified as background. End-to-end repro: a 10x3 image with dark text #202020 (2 px/row) on light bg #E8E8E8 (8 px/row), glyph_delta=0.08, adaptive=True yields foreground_color=#E8E8E8 (should be #202020), background_color=#202020 (should be #E8E8E8), glyph_pixels=24 (should be 6). The reported foreground/background colors and per-band glyph counts are inverted for the single most common real-world case (dark text on a light screenshot). The contrast ratio number happens to stay correct only because contrast_ratio is symmetric, so the bug is masked in the numeric ratio but corrupts every color/luminance/glyph-count field in the report. The existing test (test_estimate_raster_text_contrast_adaptive_threshold) only asserts 0.0 < threshold < 1.0, which does not catch this.
fix: Return the inter-class boundary instead of the included-bin center. Either (a) report the bin edge: `best_t = lo + (i + 1) / bins * span` at the winning bin, or (b) report the midpoint of the two class means: `best_t = (mean0 + mean1) / 2` captured at the winning iteration. Option (b) is most robust for the downstream `abs(lum - adaptive_t)` split. Add a regression test asserting _otsu_threshold([0.2]*100+[0.8]*100) is within ~0.1 of 0.5, and an end-to-end dark-text-on-light test asserting foreground_color is the dark color.

verdict.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. I read src/term_chameleon/text_contrast.py lines 66-101 and confirmed the code logic: at line 100, best_t = t_i, where t_i (line 93) is the bin CENTER of the last bin assigned to class 0, not the inter-class boundary/valley. This is a genuine defect in the Otsu implementation — standard Otsu must return the separating threshold, but this returns a value that sits inside the dark cluster whenever the dark cluster is contained in class 0.

NUMERIC COUNTEREXAMPLES — all reproduced by running the actual code:
- _otsu_threshold([0.2]*100 + [0.8]*100) = 0.201171875 (valley should be ~0.5)
- _otsu_threshold([0.0]*50 + [1.0]*50) = 0.001953125 (should be ~0.5)
- _otsu_threshold([0.9]*900 + [0.02]*100) = 0.021718750 (should be ~0.46)

END-TO-END — reproduced exactly with a 10x3 image, dark text #202020 (2px/row) on light bg #E8E8E8 (8px/row), glyph_delta=0.08, adaptive=True: foreground_color=#E8E8E8, background_color=#202020, glyph_pixels=24, band glyph count=24. This is the claimed inversion: the light background is classified as glyph and the dark text as background, because split_fn (line 190-191) computes abs(lum - adaptive_t) >= glyph_delta, and with adaptive_t≈0.02 sitting on the dark cluster, the dark text pixels are NEAR the threshold (classified background) while the light pixels are FAR (classified glyph). foreground_color/background_color/foreground_luminance/background_luminance and per-band glyph_pixels counts are all corrupted for the single most common real-world case (dark text on light screenshot).

MASKING confirmed: contrast_ratio is symmetric so the contrast number (13.30 in my repro) stays correct, hiding the bug in the headline metric. The existing test test_estimate_raster_text_contrast_adaptive_threshold (tests/test_text_contrast.py:33-47) only asserts 0.0 < threshold < 1.0 and uses a symmetric white-on-black fixture where the lighter color is defined as the foreground glyph — so the inversion is invisible there (threshold ≈ 0.00195, white-far=glyph happens to equal the asserted fg=#FFFFFF). It does not catch the dark-text-on-light failure.

FIX validated: I implemented the proposed option (b) best_t = (mean0 + mean1) / 2 at the winning iteration; it yields 0.46 for the dark-text case and 0.5 for the symmetric case — the correct valley values.

NOVELTY: This is distinct from the round-1 fixed issue at the same line range (test_background_fallback_uses_set_based_lookup, a separate O(n)->O(1) fallback-lookup fix). This bug is about the Otsu return value being a bin center instead of the boundary — a different defect.

SEVERITY: I keep HIGH rather than CRITICAL. It is a real correctness bug that silently inverts color/luminance/glyph-count reporting for the most common input, but it is in a diagnostic/estimation reporting path (not security or data-loss), and the headline contrast ratio remains correct, so downstream pass/fail decisions are unaffected. That bounds the blast radius to the descriptive fields of the report.

title: watch-live daemon accumulates an unbounded in-memory events list (RAM growth to GB over daemon lifetime)
description: run_watch_live appends one WatchLiveEvent to a local `events` list every sample iteration and only returns the full list to the caller after the loop exits. The installed AutoLaunch daemon command hardcodes `--duration 315360000` (10 years) in watch_daemon.watch_live_command (watch_daemon.py:124-126), so the loop effectively never terminates. At the default 10s interval that is 8,640 events/day and 3,153,600 events/year, each WatchLiveEvent carrying string fields (reason, message, screenshot source path). The list is never trimmed or flushed, so the daemon's RSS grows without bound for the entire time iTerm2 stays open — hundreds of MB to multiple GB over weeks/months. Trigger: install the watch daemon and leave a terminal open for days.
fix: Do not collect events into an unbounded list for the long-running case. Stream each event out as it is produced (yield from a generator, or invoke an on_event callback that prints/logs immediately), and have the CLI consume the stream. Keep at most a bounded ring buffer if a return value is still needed for tests.

verdict.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL and genuinely NEW (not among the 27 round-1 findings in docs/audit-findings-2026-06-27.json; AUDIT_SUMMARY.md even explicitly claims "no memory leaks").

Code confirmation (all cited lines accurate):
- src/term_chameleon/watch_live.py:143 declares `events: list[WatchLiveEvent] = []`.
- Line 146 `while True:` loops; the only exit is the duration check at lines 148 (`now - start > config.duration and events`) and 202 (`clock() - start >= config.duration`).
- Lines 186-200 `events.append(WatchLiveEvent(...))` every iteration; line 206 `return events` only after the loop ends. There is no clear/trim/flush/ring-buffer anywhere (grep confirms no `events.clear`, `del`, slicing, or max-iteration guard).
- watch_daemon.py:124-126 hardcodes `--duration 315360000` (= 315360000/86400 = 3650 days ≈ 10 years), with default `interval=10.0` (line 107). So the loop effectively never terminates.
- The CLI consumer (cli.py:1129-1130) does `events = run_watch_live(config_obj)` then iterates only AFTER it returns — so the daemon process holds the entire growing list in RAM and never even reaches the print loop for the lifetime of the session.

Arithmetic check: 86400/10 = 8,640 events/day and ×365 = 3,153,600 events/year (claim's numbers correct). Each WatchLiveEvent is an 11-field frozen dataclass carrying 3+ strings (reason, message, candidate_mode/mode); measured ~300-550 bytes/event including string payloads and list overhead, yielding roughly several hundred MB to ~1-2 GB/year of monotonic, unbounded RSS growth. The growth is genuinely unbounded: nothing restarts or flushes the single long-lived process (the autolaunch script starts it once per iTerm2 launch and uses a PID file only to avoid duplicates, watch_daemon.py:180-200).

The proposed fix (stream events via generator/callback for the long-running case, keep at most a bounded ring buffer for tests) is sound and directly addresses the root cause.

Severity adjustment HIGH -> MEDIUM: the claim's mechanism and trajectory are correct, but real-world impact is a slow leak, not an acute failure. At ~300-550 bytes/event it takes weeks-to-months of a continuously open terminal to reach the hundreds-of-MB-to-GB range the claim cites (~1-2 GB/year, not multiple GB in days/weeks). It is a resource/maintainability defect with no correctness, data-loss, or security consequence, and only manifests in the installed-daemon long-running configuration (the interactive `watch-live` CLI defaults to duration=60s and is fine). Real, new, and worth fixing, but MEDIUM rather than HIGH.

title: watch-live daemon never emits output until the (never-ending) loop returns — log file stays empty, no observability
description: run_watch_live performs no print/log/flush during the loop (grep for print/log/flush in watch_live.py returns nothing); all output happens in cli.py:_watch_live (lines 1130-1139) only AFTER run_watch_live returns. The AutoLaunch script redirects the child's stdout/stderr to ~/Library/Logs/term-chameleon-watch-live.log (watch_daemon.py:193-199), but because the daemon loop runs for 315360000s and never returns, the log file is written with nothing for the entire session. Operators have zero visibility into whether the daemon is healthy, what it is sampling, or why a switch did/didn't apply. Combined with the buffered events list, a crash loses all history.
fix: Emit each event incrementally (print with flush=True or use the logging module writing to the log path) from inside the loop, independent of loop termination. This also resolves the unbounded-list growth if events are streamed rather than accumulated.

verdict.reasoning:
VERIFIED and NEW. All cited facts confirmed against source.

watch_live.py:123-206 — run_watch_live() runs `while True`, appends each result to a local `events: list[WatchLiveEvent]` (line 186), and only `return events` at the end (line 206). grep -nE "print|logging|log\.|flush|sys\.std" over watch_live.py returns nothing (exit code 1), confirming zero in-loop output. The loop only breaks when `now - start > config.duration` (line 148) or `clock() - start >= config.duration` (line 202).

cli.py:1129-1139 — output happens ONLY after run_watch_live() returns: `events = run_watch_live(config_obj)` then a `for event in events: print(...)` loop, then `print("[ok] watch-live completed ...")`. Nothing is emitted while the loop runs.

watch_daemon.py:125-126 — the daemon command is `watch-live --yes --duration 315360000` (~10 years), so for the daemon deployment the duration guard is effectively never satisfied and run_watch_live never returns. watch_daemon.py:193-199 — the generated AutoLaunch main() opens LOG_PATH ("~/Library/Logs/term-chameleon-watch-live.log" per line 19) with buffering=0 and redirects the child's stdout/stderr there. Because the child's stdout output only fires post-return, the log file receives nothing for the entire daemon session. The observability gap is real: an operator tailing the log sees an empty file and cannot tell if the daemon is healthy, sampling, or switching. The "crash loses all history" follow-on is also accurate since events live only in memory until return.

NEWNESS: Cross-checked against all 37 round-1 findings in docs/audit-findings-2026-06-27.json. The watch_live.py/watch_daemon.py findings cover (a) cooldown discarding debounce candidate state, (b) private ModeSelector state mutation/encapsulation, (c) non-atomic PID write / duplicate-watcher race, (d) PID PermissionError handling, (e) validate_executable test breakage, (f) uninstall dry_run contradiction. NONE address the no-incremental-output / empty-log / observability concern. This is a genuinely new issue.

SEVERITY ADJUSTMENT (HIGH -> MEDIUM): The claim's mechanics are fully correct, but it is an operability/observability defect, not a correctness, data-loss, or security bug. The watcher still samples and switches modes correctly; the only impact is that the long-running daemon's log stays empty (and in-memory event history is volatile). For an interactive finite-duration `watch-live` invocation, output does appear at the end, so impact is confined to the daemon deployment. That makes it a real maintainability/operability gap worth fixing (stream events via logging/flush from inside the loop) but below HIGH. Note the claim's secondary "unbounded events list growth" is a distinct memory concern (~31M objects over 10y at 10s) that the proposed streaming fix would coincidentally also resolve; the observability claim itself stands independently.

title: Stale PID file is never removed on watcher exit, so PID reuse makes the guard permanently refuse to start
description: The AutoLaunch script writes process.pid to PID_PATH but nothing ever removes the PID file when the watch-live child exits (crash, kill, or normal stop). pid_is_running uses os.kill(pid,0), which only checks that *some* process with that PID exists. After the watcher dies and the OS recycles its PID to an unrelated process, _existing_watcher_running() returns True for the stale PID and the daemon silently refuses to start on the next iTerm2 launch — the watcher appears installed and 'running' (watch-daemon-status would also report running) but does nothing.
fix: Have the watch-live process itself own the PID file: write its own getpid() at startup and remove it via atexit/finally on exit. Optionally record a start timestamp or validate the process command line before trusting the PID, to defend against PID reuse.

verdict.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading src/term_chameleon/watch_daemon.py. The generated AutoLaunch wrapper writes the child PID at line 200 (PID_PATH.write_text(str(process.pid) + "\n")) but nothing — not the wrapper, not the watch_live.py child (grep found zero PID/atexit/getpid references in watch_live.py), not any other module — ever removes the PID file when the watcher exits. The only unlink calls in the codebase are for artifacts, the autolaunch script (uninstall), and temp files; none target the PID file on watcher death.

_pid_is_running (lines 170-177) and the module-level pid_is_running (lines 57-64) use os.kill(pid, 0), which only verifies that *some* process holds that PID (PermissionError is even treated as running). _existing_watcher_running() (180-185) therefore returns True for a stale PID once the OS recycles it to an unrelated process, and main() (189-190) returns early, silently refusing to launch the watcher. get_watch_daemon_status() (line 251) would likewise report running=True. So the documented failure mode is reproducible as written.

Genuinely NEW: the round-1 fix af8a318 ('resolve 26 audit findings ... watch daemon') touched watch_daemon.py, but its diff contains zero pid/kill/stale/running/unlink/atexit lines — the PID-lifecycle issue was not among the 27 fixed.

Severity calibration: real bug, but the *permanent* refusal only triggers on actual PID reuse landing on the exact recycled value, which on macOS (sequential PID assignment, high wrap ceiling) is probabilistic rather than guaranteed. The common case is a stale PID pointing at nothing (returns False, harmless) or the live watcher still owning it. The 'permanently refuses to start' phrasing overstates likelihood, but the staleness/no-cleanup defect and its reuse hazard are genuine. MEDIUM is appropriate (maintainability/correctness concern with conditional impact, no security or data-loss dimension). The proposed fix (child owns the PID file via getpid + atexit/finally, plus optional start-time/cmdline validation against PID reuse) is sound.

title: Artifact prune cap counts analysis files, halving effective sample retention; dead analysis-deletion branch
description: _prune_artifacts globs 'sample-*.png', which matches BOTH sample-NNNN.png and the downsampled sample-NNNN-analysis.png (verified: both match). So WATCH_MAX_ARTIFACTS=200 actually retains ~100 real samples, not 200 (verified: pushing 250 iterations leaves 100 samples + 100 analysis files). Worse, the analysis-deletion branch (lines 117-119) is incorrect/dead: when the popped 'oldest' is itself an analysis file, it computes oldest.stem+'-analysis' -> 'sample-NNNN-analysis-analysis.png', which never exists, so it relies purely on mtime interleaving to pair files. The cap semantics are wrong and the cleanup is fragile.
fix: Glob only real samples (exclude '-analysis' via a stricter pattern or filter), count the cap against real samples, and when deleting a sample also delete its paired analysis file explicitly. Drop the broken analysis-pop branch.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against src/term_chameleon/watch_live.py:108-120 and confirmed empirically.

1) GLOB OVER-COUNTS (CONFIRMED): Samples are named `sample-{index:04d}.png` (line 74) and analysis files `sample-NNNN-analysis.png` (line 93 / 117). The prune glob `output_dir.glob("sample-*.png")` (line 110) matches BOTH. Empirical test: glob over `sample-0001.png` + `sample-0001-analysis.png` returns both files.

2) CAP HALVED (CONFIRMED): Simulated 250 iterations, each writing a sample + analysis file then calling the real prune logic with WATCH_MAX_ARTIFACTS=200. Result: 200 total files retained = exactly 100 real samples + 100 analysis files. Effective real-sample retention is half the configured cap, exactly as claimed.

3) DEAD ANALYSIS BRANCH (CONFIRMED): When the popped `oldest` is itself an analysis file, `oldest.with_name(f"{oldest.stem}-analysis.png")` produces `sample-NNNN-analysis-analysis.png`, which never exists (verified: `.exists()` returns False). So lines 117-119 do nothing in that case. The branch only ever fires for real-sample pops. Pairing therefore relies on mtime interleaving rather than the branch logic.

NUANCE / SEVERITY: The dead branch does NOT cause orphaned files or a disk leak in practice. Because each iteration writes the sample then its analysis (adjacent mtimes), analysis files still get pruned — they are simply counted and unlinked directly as glob entries rather than via the paired-deletion path. So the real-world consequence is purely "the cap retains ~half as many real debug samples as the WATCH_MAX_ARTIFACTS value implies" plus dead/fragile cleanup code. No data loss, no security impact, debug-artifact only. MEDIUM (correctness/semantics bug) is defensible; it leans toward LOW given the artifacts are non-user-facing debug screenshots.

NEWNESS: The prune function was introduced in commit b1d8e76 and its body is byte-identical to the current version — never amended. grep of the audit/edge-case/stress-test docs (AUDIT_SUMMARY.md, EDGE_CASE_FINDINGS_SUMMARY.md, STRESS_TEST_FINDINGS.md) for prune/max_artifacts/halv/sample-* found no prior mention of this glob-over-count or dead-branch issue. Not among the round-1 fixes; genuinely new.

title: WATCH_MAX_ARTIFACTS bound is silently halved and prunes analysis files incoherently
description: _prune_artifacts globs `sample-*.png` (line 110) to cap retained samples at WATCH_MAX_ARTIFACTS=200. But each iteration also writes a downscaled companion named `sample-NNNN-analysis.png` (line 93 in _analysis_image_path), and `sample-*.png` matches `sample-NNNN-analysis.png` too (verified via fnmatch). So the 200-file cap counts companions, effectively retaining only ~100 real samples, and the per-file companion-cleanup logic at lines 117-120 is incoherent: when `oldest` is itself an analysis file, oldest.stem='sample-0001-analysis' derives the nonexistent 'sample-0001-analysis-analysis.png', so the branch is a no-op; conversely a real sample can be unlinked while its already-counted analysis sibling is removed separately. Net effect: the directory is still bounded, but at half the intended size and with confusing/dead cleanup code. Not a leak, but a correctness/maintainability defect in the resource-bounding logic.
fix: Glob only true samples with a stricter pattern (e.g. regex or `glob('sample-[0-9]*.png')` then filter out names ending in '-analysis'), prune the matched samples to max_artifacts, and unlink each sample's '-analysis' companion alongside it. This makes the bound match its name and removes the dead branch.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against src/term_chameleon/watch_live.py.

Confirmed constants: WATCH_MAX_ARTIFACTS=200 (line 20), WATCH_ANALYSIS_MAX_DIMENSION=700 (line 19). _analysis_image_path (lines 89-105) writes a downscaled companion named `sample-NNNN-analysis.png` via sips when available. _prune_artifacts (lines 108-120) globs `sample-*.png` (line 110).

Core claim VERIFIED via fnmatch and empirical simulation:
- `sample-*.png` matches both `sample-0001.png` AND `sample-0001-analysis.png` (fnmatch.fnmatch returns True for both). So the companions are counted against the 200-file cap.
- Simulated 150 iterations (150 real + 150 companions = 300 files matched), cap 200 → result: 200 total = exactly 100 real samples + 100 companions. The bound is silently halved relative to its name, as claimed.
- Directory remains bounded (no leak) — confirmed; it stops at exactly max_artifacts. The claim explicitly states "Not a leak" which is correct.

Cleanup-incoherence claim VERIFIED:
- When `oldest` is itself an analysis file, oldest.stem='sample-0001-analysis' so the derived path is 'sample-0001-analysis-analysis.png' which never exists → that branch (lines 117-120) is a dead no-op. Confirmed by Path.stem/with_name test.
- Additional incoherence reproduced via step trace: because samples and their companions have adjacent mtimes, the sorted glob interleaves them. Popping a real sample fires the branch and unlinks its companion, but that companion is STILL in the `files` list and gets popped (already-deleted, missing_ok no-op) on the next iteration. So each pair is double-processed — the companion is both counted in the cap and separately unlinked. This matches the claim's "a real sample can be unlinked while its already-counted analysis sibling is removed separately."

The claim's technical assertions all hold. The proposed fix (glob true samples only via a stricter pattern, prune to max, and unlink each companion alongside) is sound and would make the bound match its name and remove the dead branch.

Genuinely NEW: this concerns the specific glob/cap/companion interaction in _prune_artifacts, a self-contained correctness/maintainability defect not implied by the description as a previously-fixed item.

Severity: real but low impact — the directory stays bounded (no resource leak), watch_live is a diagnostic tool, and the only effects are retaining ~half the intended samples plus confusing/dead cleanup code. This sits at the LOW/MEDIUM boundary; I keep MEDIUM as the claimed severity is defensible for a correctness bug in resource-bounding logic with dead code, though it leans LOW on user-facing impact.

title: release-check --live-stage drives Safari and iTerm2 with no --yes safety gate (bypasses the GUI-mutation confirmation the live-stage command enforces)
description: The dedicated `live-stage` subcommand refuses to drive GUI apps without --yes: `_live_stage` (cli.py:1335-1339) returns exit 2 with 'Refusing to drive GUI apps without --yes' unless --yes or --dry-run is given. But `release-check --live-stage` reaches the SAME GUI-driving code with no such gate: `_release_check` (cli.py:461-473) forwards live_stage=args.live_stage into run_release_check(), which at release_check.py:80-88 calls run_live_stage(dry_run=False, capture=True) unconditionally. run_live_stage at live_stage.py:165-168 then executes run_osascript(browser_script) and run_osascript(iterm_script), which drive Safari and iTerm2 (move/resize windows, load a background, run a pattern script). Concrete trigger: `term-chameleon release-check --live-stage` (no --yes) physically manipulates the user's Safari and iTerm2 windows, while `term-chameleon live-stage` (no --yes) refuses to. The safety contract is inconsistent across the two entry points.
fix: Gate the live-stage portion of release-check behind a confirmation flag too. Either add a --yes requirement to `release-check --live-stage` (mirroring _live_stage), or pass an explicit confirmation flag down so run_release_check only runs run_live_stage with dry_run=False when the user confirmed. Update the README (line 95) to document the confirmation requirement.

verdict.reasoning:
Every cited line was verified against the real code. The `release-check` subparser (cli.py:205-226) defines no `--yes` flag, and `_release_check` (cli.py:934-970) performs no confirmation check before calling `run_release_check(..., live_stage=live_stage)` (cli.py:947-958). In release_check.py:80-88, `if live_stage:` unconditionally calls `run_live_stage(..., dry_run=False)`. In live_stage.py:165-168, `if not dry_run:` executes `run_osascript(browser_script)` then `run_osascript(iterm_script)`. The scripts (live_stage.py:71-125) genuinely drive GUI apps: Safari is activated, opens a location, and has its window resized; iTerm2 is activated, creates a new window, resizes it, and writes text. By contrast, the dedicated `_live_stage` handler (cli.py:1335-1339) hard-refuses with exit 2 ("Refusing to drive GUI apps without --yes") unless `--yes` or `--dry-run` is given. So the safety contract is genuinely inconsistent across the two entry points: `term-chameleon live-stage` refuses, while `term-chameleon release-check --live-stage` proceeds to manipulate the user's Safari and iTerm2 windows with no confirmation.

Genuinely NEW: git history shows round-1 fixes landed in af8a318 ("resolve 26 audit findings") and c37328e. The af8a318 diff did not touch release_check.py at all, and its only cli.py change was adding an unrelated unhandled-command guard. The `--yes` gate on `_live_stage` was added in an earlier commit (83778379), and that gate was never extended to the release-check path. So this inconsistency was not among the 27 round-1 issues.

Severity note: the trigger requires the user to explicitly opt in via the `--live-stage` flag (not silent/accidental), and the impact is focus-stealing GUI window manipulation rather than data loss or a security breach. That makes HIGH slightly generous; MEDIUM is the better fit, though it remains a legitimate bug because it bypasses an explicit safety gate the project itself enforces on the sibling command.

title: Malformed iTerm color silently disables all contrast diagnostics (regression from the color.py + iterm_profile.py fix pair)
description: Commit af8a318 made Color.from_iterm_dict raise ValueError on a non-numeric component (color.py:40-41), then wrapped the call in ItermProfile.color() with `except (ValueError, TypeError): return None` (iterm_profile.py:53-56). The combined effect is a silent-pass regression in diagnose(): every contrast check is guarded by `if bg and fg:` / `if bg and ansiN:` (diagnostics.py:73-94), so when Background Color is a present-but-malformed dict, color() returns None and ALL LOW_*_CONTRAST checks are skipped, yielding a clean report for an unrenderable/corrupt profile. Verified by counterexample: a profile with Background Color {"Red Component":"oops",...} and black Foreground produces zero LOW_*_CONTRAST diagnostics, whereas the same profile with a valid black Background correctly produces LOW_FOREGROUND_CONTRAST. Before the fix (commit 158a8a9), color() let the exception propagate to the CLI handler, which printed `error: ...` and exited 2 — the user was told the profile was bad. Now the corruption is hidden behind a falsely-clean accessibility report, which directly undermines the stated goal of the sibling contrast.py fix in the same commit (avoid misleading PASS/FAIL accessibility results).
fix: Distinguish 'key absent' (return None, legitimately optional) from 'key present but unparseable' (surface it). Option A: in diagnose(), when profile.profile.get('Background Color') is a dict but color('Background Color') is None, emit a FAIL diagnostic (e.g. MALFORMED_BACKGROUND_COLOR) instead of silently skipping. Option B: have color() only swallow the case where the dict is genuinely color-shaped-but-out-of-range, and let truly malformed dicts (non-numeric component) raise, so the CLI reports the corrupt profile rather than passing it.

verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED and reproduced. Every link in the claim's causal chain checks out against the real code, and the empirical counterexample matches exactly.

Mechanism (verified file-by-file):
- color.py:32-41: `Color.from_iterm_dict` raises ValueError on a malformed component. For a string like "oops", `float("oops")` raises ValueError directly; for list/dict/None components, the TypeError is converted to ValueError at color.py:40-41. I confirmed all four malformed kinds (str, list, dict, None) surface as ValueError.
- iterm_profile.py:50-57: `color()` wraps the call in `except (ValueError, TypeError): return None`, so ALL malformed components silently become None.
- diagnostics.py:73-115: every contrast check is guarded by `if bg and fg:` / `if bg and ansiN:` / `if selection and selected:`. When bg is None, every LOW_*_CONTRAST check is skipped.

Empirical counterexample (run, not reasoned): a profile with Background Color {"Red Component":"oops",...} + valid black Foreground produced `color('Background Color') = None` and ZERO diagnostics (clean report, exit 0). The same profile with a valid black Background correctly produced ['LOW_FOREGROUND_CONTRAST']. This is precisely the claim's stated counterexample.

Before/after regression confirmed: at commit 158a8a9 (pre-fix), `color()` was `return Color.from_iterm_dict(value)` with no try/except, so the ValueError propagated out of diagnose() → _doctor() → the CLI dispatch handler at cli.py:584-586 (`except (ValueError, OSError, ...): print(f"error: {exc}"); return 2`). So the user previously got an explicit error+exit-2; now the corruption is hidden behind a falsely-clean accessibility report at exit 0.

Genuinely NEW (not a round-1 fix): commit af8a318's body describes the color/iterm_profile change only as "normalize float() TypeError on malformed profile JSON into ValueError" — it addressed the leak-past-CLI-handler concern but never the downstream silent-skip in diagnose(). Tests test_iterm_profile.py:30-67 deliberately assert `color()` returns None on malformed components, and NO test asserts diagnose() surfaces the malformation. The regression directly undermines the sibling contrast.py fix in the same commit, whose stated goal was to avoid "misleading PASS/FAIL accessibility results."

Severity: I assess MEDIUM rather than HIGH. The trigger requires a present-but-corrupt color dict (non-numeric component) — an uncommon real-world state, typically only from hand-edited or truly corrupt profile JSON, not from iTerm2-exported profiles. The impact (a falsely-clean accessibility report on an unrenderable profile) is real and silent, which is what keeps it above LOW, but it is not a security or data-loss issue and the affected input is an edge case. The proposed fix (distinguish key-absent from key-present-but-unparseable, e.g. emit MALFORMED_BACKGROUND_COLOR) is sound.

title: PID duplicate-guard is TOCTOU with no lock; concurrent iTerm2 launches can spawn two daemons and orphan the first
description: The generated AutoLaunch script's _existing_watcher_running() reads the PID file, checks os.kill(pid,0), then (in main) spawns subprocess.Popen and writes the new PID — a classic check-then-act with no file lock (no flock/fcntl/O_EXCL anywhere in the codebase). If iTerm2 launches the script twice close together (relaunch during shutdown, two app instances, or a manual run racing autolaunch), both invocations observe no running watcher, both spawn a watch-live process, and the second PID write clobbers the first. The first watcher's PID is then lost forever, so uninstall/stop can never terminate it; it keeps mutating the session profile and consuming CPU/RAM until the process tree dies.
fix: Use an exclusive lock to make check-and-spawn atomic: open the PID file with O_CREAT|O_EXCL or take an fcntl.flock on a lockfile before checking/spawning, and only spawn if the lock is acquired. Release/clear on child exit.

verdict.reasoning:
The code mechanics are accurate. In the generated AutoLaunch template (src/term_chameleon/watch_daemon.py:152-205), main() calls _existing_watcher_running() (lines 180-189), which reads the PID file and does os.kill(pid,0), then spawns subprocess.Popen (line 194) and writes the new PID with a non-atomic PID_PATH.write_text (line 200). There is no fcntl/flock/O_EXCL/lockf anywhere in src/ (grep returned empty), so the check-then-act window genuinely exists in code.

HOWEVER, this claim is NOT genuinely new. It is a re-statement of round-1 audit finding #26 in docs/audit-findings-2026-06-27.json (title: "Non-atomic PID file write in generated autolaunch script enables duplicate-watcher race condition", file src/term_chameleon/watch_daemon.py, lines 145-205). That finding raised the identical TOCTOU between _existing_watcher_running() and Popen+PID-write, described the identical "two iTerm2 windows opening simultaneously" concurrent-spawn scenario, and proposed the identical fix (fcntl.flock around the check-and-spawn section). It was one of the 37 confirmed round-1 findings (verdict isReal=true).

The round-1 verdict adjudicated it down to LOW with a rebuttal I independently confirmed: the generated script (term_chameleon_watch_live.py) is only ever installed into ~/Library/Application Support/iTerm2/Scripts/AutoLaunch (verified via install.py:13 DEFAULT_AUTOLAUNCH_DIR and watch_daemon.py:217), and iTerm2 AutoLaunch scripts run once per application launch, not once per window — so the claimed concurrent double-invocation has no realistic trigger in the actual deployment model. The watcher also mutates only the session-local profile, bounding blast radius. The current code still has no atomic write or lock (the round-1 commit af8a318 only touched executable validation and PID PermissionError handling, not this race), so the defect text remains true, but it is a known, already-adjudicated, marginal defensive-coding nit — not a new MEDIUM-severity duplicate-daemon hazard. The claim also overstates impact ("orphan the first watcher forever", "manual run racing autolaunch") beyond what the single-per-launch deployment supports.

Verdict: real code-level observation but a duplicate of round-1 finding #26, correctly rated LOW.

title: read_pid / pid_is_running accept 0 and negative PIDs, yielding false 'running' from os.kill(0,0) / os.kill(-1,0)
description: read_pid (line 67) does int(...) with no range check, so a corrupted/truncated PID file containing '0' or '-1' parses successfully. pid_is_running(0) calls os.kill(0,0), which per POSIX targets the *current process group* (returns True), and pid_is_running(-1) calls os.kill(-1,0), which targets every process the user may signal (returns True). Verified at runtime: pid_is_running(0)=True, pid_is_running(-1)=True. Result: a corrupted PID file silently makes the duplicate-guard report 'running' forever, preventing the watcher from ever starting. The same flaw exists in the generated AutoLaunch script's _pid_is_running (lines 170-185).
fix: In read_pid, reject non-positive PIDs (return None if pid <= 0). In pid_is_running, guard `if pid <= 0: return False` before calling os.kill. Apply the same guards to the generated script.

verdict.reasoning:
Code verified at src/term_chameleon/watch_daemon.py. read_pid (lines 67-71) does int(...) with no range check, so a PID file containing '0' or '-1' parses successfully. pid_is_running (lines 57-64) has no guard before os.kill(pid, 0). I confirmed the runtime claim by execution: pid_is_running(0)=True and pid_is_running(-1)=True (os.kill(0,0) targets the current process group and succeeds; os.kill(-1,0) targets all signalable processes and succeeds). The same flaw is duplicated in the generated AutoLaunch script (_pid_is_running lines 170-177, _existing_watcher_running reads int(...) at line 182, gating watcher startup at line 189). Both helpers also feed get_watch_daemon_status (lines 242, 251). The factual description is fully accurate and the code is unfixed in the current tree.

NOVELTY: This overlaps heavily with an existing round-1 audit finding (docs/audit-findings-2026-06-27.json, entry titled 'PID file treated as alive on PermissionError; corrupt PID indistinguishable from missing'), which already covered these exact two functions and the corrupt-PID-file scenario and adjudicated it LOW/leaning NOT_A_BUG. The round-2 claim adds a genuinely new mechanism not in round-1 (PID values 0 and -1 plus the POSIX process-group / all-processes semantics of os.kill), but it is not a wholly new area. It is also not one of the 26 already-FIXED issues — the code remains unpatched.

SEVERITY: The claim overstates impact at MEDIUM. To trigger 'running forever', the PID file must be corrupted to exactly '0' or '-1' (with optional whitespace). Any other garbage (empty/truncated/non-numeric) raises ValueError, read_pid returns None, the guard reports not-running, and the daemon starts and overwrites the file — the desired recovery. The PID file is only ever written with a real positive process.pid, so no normal path produces 0/-1. The consequence in the razor-thin triggering subset is a stuck not-started watcher (recoverable by deleting the file) — a robustness/observability nit, not data loss or security. Given near-zero trigger probability and the per-user single-launch deployment model, LOW is the appropriate severity, consistent with how round-1 graded the same functions. The proposed fix (reject pid <= 0 in read_pid and guard pid_is_running) is correct and cheap hardening.

title: Threaded iTerm2 probe leaks an event loop + open socket on timeout and mutates iterm2 module-global state off-thread
description: probe_iterm_connection runs iterm2.run_until_complete on a daemon thread and abandons it on worker.join(timeout) when it is still alive (acknowledged in the code comment). The underlying iterm2 Connection.run (connection.py) calls asyncio.new_event_loop()/set_event_loop and mutates the module-global gDisconnectCallbacks list (read-then-reset to []) with no lock. On timeout the abandoned worker keeps an open iTerm2 socket and an unclosed asyncio loop, and may later mutate gDisconnectCallbacks concurrently with any subsequent run_until_complete in the same process. For one-shot CLI use (cli.py:1182, status.py:102) this leaks at most one thread/socket per process invocation and the process exits soon after, so practical impact is low — but it is a genuine resource leak and shared-state race if the probe is ever called in a loop or alongside a live apply in the same process.
fix: Bound the probe with a real timeout on the connection/loop instead of abandoning a thread: run the coroutine via asyncio.run with asyncio.wait_for, or explicitly close the connection/loop in the worker's finally so a timed-out attempt releases its socket. Avoid relying on iterm2's global callback list from multiple threads.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against real code. iterm_connection.py:45-51 starts a daemon thread, calls worker.join(timeout), and on worker.is_alive() returns a timeout probe and abandons the thread — the code comment (lines 28-30) explicitly acknowledges "a timed-out thread is left to die with the process." The abandoned worker is blocked inside iterm2.run_until_complete -> Connection.run (connection.py:210-252), which at lines 225-226 does asyncio.new_event_loop()/set_event_loop with no finally-based close (the loop is only closed on the NEXT run() call, lines 222-223), so the loop and its open iTerm2 socket leak on the timeout path. The module-global gDisconnectCallbacks (connection.py:12) is read-then-reset to [] without a lock at lines 247-249, a genuine unsynchronized shared-state mutation if two run() calls overlap.

Caveat that the claim already handles correctly: the gDisconnectCallbacks reset executes only AFTER loop.run_until_complete returns, so a hung/timed-out probe blocked inside run_until_complete has not reached the reset; the race only materializes if a second run() runs concurrently with a completing one — exactly the "if ever called in a loop or alongside a live apply" condition the claim states.

Impact is genuinely low: both callers (cli.py:1182, status.py:102) are one-shot CLI invocations with no loop, and the process exits shortly after, so the OS reclaims the thread/socket at exit. The leak/race only surfaces on the rare timeout path (unresponsive iTerm2) in a long-lived process.

Newness: git log for this file shows only two commits — the feature add (0b14d88) and the round-1 timeout-bounding fix (c37328e). That fix is what introduced the daemon-thread-abandon-on-join pattern; no subsequent commit closes the leak/race. So this is a new follow-on issue, not a re-report of an already-fixed round-1 item.

LOW severity is appropriate: real resource leak + latent unsynchronized global mutation, but bounded to one thread/socket per one-shot process and only on an uncommon error path.

title: backup_file creates an uncapped timestamped backup on every install — unbounded file growth in AutoLaunch/profile dirs
description: install_watch_autolaunch_script calls backup_file(target) on every (re)install when the target exists (watch_daemon.py:220-221), and backup_file always writes a new .backup.<timestamp> copy with no retention limit (safe_io.py:13-26). Repeated install-watch-daemon runs (and profile installs that use the same helper) accumulate backup files indefinitely in ~/Library/.../AutoLaunch, slowly growing disk usage and cluttering the directory the daemon scans.
fix: Cap retained backups (keep the N most recent, prune older ones) or make backup creation opt-in / skip when content is unchanged (compare hashes before copying).

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against source. safe_io.py:22-27 (`backup_file`) unconditionally creates a new `.backup.<timestamp>` copy via `unique_backup_path` whenever the source exists, with no retention cap and no content-hash dedup. watch_daemon.py:220-221 calls `backup_file(target)` on every re-install when the target exists. The same helper is also used by install.py:49,107, modes.py:37, and fixes.py:83. A codebase grep for prune/retention/cleanup/max_backup/keep-recent found only `_prune_artifacts` in watch_live.py, which prunes watch *artifacts* (a different concern), not backups — so nothing caps backup files. Each (re)install therefore leaves a new backup behind permanently, and because the generated script content is deterministic for a given command/log/pid, repeated installs produce near-identical backups with no dedup. The claim's mechanism is accurate.

Severity is correctly LOW and not understated: `install-watch-daemon` is a manually invoked CLI subcommand (cli.py:99,413,738), not an automatic loop, so accumulation requires repeated manual re-runs. The files are small (a shell-style launch script / plist or profile), so disk growth is slow and the practical impact is directory clutter rather than a functional bug. This is a genuine maintainability/hygiene issue, real but minor.

Newness: it concerns backup retention specifically, which is untouched by the only existing pruning code (artifact pruning in watch_live.py), so it is not subsumed by that mechanism. Treated as a new, distinct LOW finding.

title: Backup files accumulate without bound across install/uninstall/fix/mode writes
description: backup_file() always creates a new timestamped `<name>.backup.<YYYYMMDDTHHMMSS.ffffff>` copy (unique_backup_path even appends a .N counter to avoid collisions) and nothing in the codebase ever prunes these. It is called on every profile fix (fixes.py:83), mode write (modes.py:37), install (install.py:49, install.py:107), watch-daemon install (watch_daemon.py:221) and uninstall (watch_daemon.py:266). Repeatedly running `fix`/`apply`/`install`/`uninstall` leaves an ever-growing pile of backup copies next to the target (e.g. in the iTerm2 DynamicProfiles dir and ~/Library autolaunch dir) with no retention policy. Low severity (small text files), but it is unbounded growth with no cleanup path.
fix: Add a retention policy to backup_file (keep the most recent N, e.g. 5, deleting older `<name>.backup.*` matches), or expose a prune step. Keep deletions best-effort (suppress OSError) as elsewhere.

verdict.reasoning: VERIFIED against actual code. safe_io.py:11-27: unique_backup_path() builds `<name>.backup.<YYYYMMDDTHHMMSS.ffffff>` and appends `.N` if that path exists, so every call produces a brand-new file. backup_file() unconditionally calls unique_backup_path() then shutil.copy2 (when source exists), so it never overwrites or reuses backups. All six cited call sites confirmed verbatim: fixes.py:83, modes.py:37, install.py:49, install.py:107, watch_daemon.py:221, watch_daemon.py:266 (uninstall, when backup=True). A grep for prune/retention/unlink/cleanup across src found only _prune_artifacts in watch_live.py, which exclusively globs `sample-*.png` watch screenshots and their `-analysis.png` siblings — it never touches `.backup.*` files. There is therefore no retention or cleanup path for backups anywhere in the codebase, confirming unbounded growth. Severity LOW is correct: backed-up targets are small text files (iTerm2 DynamicProfiles plists, ~/Library autolaunch plists, shell scripts), so accumulation is slow and non-destructive — a housekeeping/maintainability concern, not a functional, data-loss, or security bug. Novelty: this is a backup-retention concern, distinct from the round-1 items visible in commit history (except-narrowing, public setter_mappings, osc/safe_io test additions, adaptive thresholding, cross-terminal support). Adding tests to safe_io is unrelated to adding a pruning policy, so this is a genuinely new issue. The proposed fix (keep most-recent N, best-effort deletion suppressing OSError to match existing style) is reasonable and consistent with the existing _prune_artifacts pattern.

title: background_html.open_file launches `open` with no timeout
description: open_file() runs `subprocess.run(['open', ...], capture_output=True)` with no timeout, unlike every other subprocess call in the project (screenshot.py, iterm_window.py, live_stage.py, watch_live.py all pass timeout=). macOS `open` normally returns immediately, but when Launch Services is wedged or a GUI app is mid-launch and unresponsive it can block; with capture_output=True the parent then waits forever on the captured pipes. Inconsistent with the rest of the codebase and a potential indefinite stall on a degraded system.
fix: Add a timeout (e.g. timeout=10) and handle subprocess.TimeoutExpired by returning a CompletedProcess-equivalent failure, matching the pattern used in iterm_window._raw_iterm_window_bounds_points.

verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED. The cited code at background_html.py:98-99 matches exactly: `subprocess.run(["open", str(Path(path))], check=False, text=True, capture_output=True)` with no timeout argument.

The inconsistency claim is verified by grep: every other blocking subprocess.run in src/ passes a timeout — watch_live.py:99 (timeout=20), live_stage.py:134 (timeout=15 default), iterm_window.py:57 & :81 (timeout=10), screenshot.py:33 (timeout=10 default). The only other timeout-less call is watch_daemon.py:194, which uses Popen and is intentionally non-blocking, so it does not contradict the claim. This open_file is the sole blocking call missing a timeout.

The blocking mechanism is technically sound: with capture_output=True, subprocess.run reads the child's stdout/stderr pipes until EOF; with no timeout it can wait indefinitely if the spawned process (or a child inheriting the pipes) holds them open while wedged. macOS `open` normally returns immediately, but a degraded Launch Services / mid-launch unresponsive GUI app is a plausible (if uncommon) stall scenario.

The proposed-fix reference is accurate: iterm_window.py:59 already does `except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:`, so the codebase has an established pattern to mirror.

Reachability confirmed: cli.py:1292 calls open_file from _background_html when open_browser is set (the `background-html --open` path).

Severity: LOW is correct, not higher. Normal-case `open` returns instantly; the hang requires a degraded system. It is a single user-invoked CLI command (interruptible with Ctrl-C), not an automated/hot path, with no data-loss or security impact. It is a robustness/consistency hardening nit.

Newness: This targets background_html.open_file, distinct from the round-1 fix areas in the commit log (osc/safe_io tests, setter_mappings, except narrowing, adaptive thresholding, cross-terminal). The fact that all other subprocess calls already carry timeouts strongly implies a prior hardening pass added them and missed this one-liner — making this a genuinely new, leftover instance rather than a re-report of an already-fixed issue.

title: setup --live cannot override config live=true (README claims explicit CLI flags override config values)
description: README line 97 states: 'watch-live, install-watch-daemon, and setup accept --config; explicit CLI flags override config values.' For `setup`, the live flag is combined as `live=live or config_live` (cli.py:1009, with config_live read at 1005). Because --live is argparse store_true, the CLI can only set it True, never explicitly False. So if a config has [setup] live = true, there is no CLI flag to turn live probing off — the config value cannot be overridden, contradicting the README precedence claim. (The watch/daemon region/iterm_window cases provide escape hatches like --whole-screen / mutually exclusive groups; setup's --live has none.)
fix: Either document the boolean-toggle exception in the README precedence statement, or replace --live with a tri-state (e.g. --live/--no-live via argparse.BooleanOptionalAction) so an explicit CLI value can override config live=true.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code. cli.py:1005 reads `config_live = bool_value(value(setup_cfg, "live"), False)` and line 1009 passes `live=live or config_live` exactly as claimed. The `setup --live` argument is defined at cli.py:246 as `action="store_true"`, so the CLI can only set it True; there is no `--no-live` counterpart. Therefore `False or config_live == config_live` (CLI omitted) and `True or config_live == True` (CLI present) — a config value of `[setup] live = true` can never be turned off from the CLI. README line 97 literally states "explicit CLI flags override config values," which is an overgeneralization for setup's boolean live flag (override only works one direction: off->on, never on->off).

I confirmed `[setup] live` is a real, validated config field (config.py:164 lists it in the setup section; config.py:203 validates it as bool; config.py:41 EXAMPLE_CONFIG shows `live = false`). I also confirmed the comparison to watch/daemon is accurate: watch-live uses a mutually-exclusive group (--region / --iterm-window) and install-watch-daemon offers --iterm-window / --whole-screen, both of which are genuine bidirectional override mechanisms, whereas setup's --live has none. watch-live has no `live` config field, so this precedence gap is unique to the setup subcommand.

The claim is technically accurate and represents a genuine documentation-vs-behavior discrepancy. It is NEW in that it is a precedence/doc nuance specific to setup's boolean flag, not a generic functional bug. Severity is correctly LOW: the practical impact is minor (a user who set live=true presumably wants live probing; the only effect is being unable to suppress it via CLI for one run, and live probing failure mainly affects the exit code, not output correctness). The proposed fixes (document the boolean exception, or use argparse.BooleanOptionalAction for a tri-state --live/--no-live) are both valid and idiomatic.

title: watch-daemon-status --json omits the 'healthy' field that the human-readable output and exit code are based on
description: The human-readable branch prints an [ok]/[warn] line driven by status.healthy and the function returns `0 if status.healthy else 1` (cli.py:810-814). The --json branch does `json.dumps(asdict(status), ...)`. Because WatchDaemonStatus.healthy is a @property, not a dataclass field (watch_daemon.py:43-46), asdict() does not include it. Verified: `watch-daemon-status --json` emits target/installed/executable/log_path/log_exists/pid_path/pid/running but no 'healthy' key, forcing JSON consumers to recompute installed&&executable themselves and risking drift from the canonical definition.
fix: Add 'healthy' explicitly to the JSON payload (e.g. build a dict that includes asdict(status) plus {'healthy': status.healthy}) so machine consumers see the same derived status the human output and exit code use.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified empirically and by source reading.

Structural facts confirmed:
- watch_daemon.py:34-47: WatchDaemonStatus is a frozen dataclass with 8 fields (target, installed, executable, log_path, log_exists, pid_path, pid, running). `healthy` is defined as a @property (lines 45-47: `return self.installed and self.executable`), NOT a dataclass field.
- cli.py:798-799: the --json branch does `print(json.dumps(asdict(status), indent=2, default=str))`.
- cli.py:801-813: the human-readable branch prints an [ok]/[warn] line driven by `status.healthy`.
- cli.py:814: `return 0 if status.healthy else 1` — the exit code is the canonical healthy definition.

Empirical reproduction: constructed a WatchDaemonStatus(installed=True, executable=True, ...) and ran asdict(). The resulting dict keys are exactly ['executable','installed','log_exists','log_path','pid','pid_path','running','target'] with 'healthy' in d == False, while s.healthy == True. dataclasses.asdict only serializes fields, never properties, so the JSON payload omits the derived 'healthy' value that both the human output and exit code rely on. This is the documented Python behavior and exactly what the claim states.

JSON consumers therefore must recompute installed&&executable themselves, risking drift from the canonical definition — a real (if low-severity) machine-readable-output inconsistency.

Newness: The round-1 audit fix was commit af8a318 ('fix: resolve 26 audit findings ... and watch daemon'). git show of that commit on cli.py shows it did not touch the _watch_daemon_status JSON branch or the healthy field. Tests only assert status.healthy at the dataclass level (test_watch_daemon.py:63,75); no test asserts 'healthy' appears in the daemon --json payload. So this is a genuinely new finding, not one of the already-fixed issues.

Severity: LOW is appropriate. It is a machine-readable-API completeness/consistency gap, not a correctness or security bug. The human output and exit code remain correct; only the JSON consumer is forced to re-derive a value. The proposed fix (merge asdict(status) with {'healthy': status.healthy}) is correct and minimal.

title: Percentile sampling clusters overlap when percentile=0.5 on small pixel sets, double-counting middle pixels
description: sample_size = max(1, round(len(pixels) * percentile)); dark = pixels[:sample_size]; light = pixels[-sample_size:]. The validation at line 44 permits percentile up to 0.5 inclusive. When 2*sample_size > len(pixels), the dark and light slices overlap and the same mid-luminance pixel(s) are averaged into BOTH the dark and light cluster, biasing both means toward the middle and understating contrast. Concrete trigger: percentile=0.5 with a 3-pixel image -> sample_size=round(1.5)=2, dark=pixels[:2], light=pixels[-2:] share index 1. Real images rarely hit this, so severity is low, but it is reachable via the public API.
fix: Cap sample_size so the two clusters cannot overlap, e.g. `sample_size = max(1, min(round(len(pixels) * percentile), len(pixels) // 2))`. Add a test with percentile=0.5 on a tiny image asserting dark/light pixel sets are disjoint.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading the real code and reproducing the bug. At src/term_chameleon/pixel_contrast.py:70-72, sample_size = max(1, round(len(pixels) * percentile)); dark = pixels[:sample_size]; light = pixels[-sample_size:]. Validation at line 44 permits percentile up to 0.5 inclusive via the public estimate_image_contrast API. The cited trigger is exact: with len=3 and percentile=0.5, round(1.5)=2, so dark=indices[0,1] and light=indices[1,2] overlap at index 1. I reproduced this live: a 3-pixel image (black/mid-gray/white) at percentile=0.5 yields dark_color #404040 and light_color #C0C0C0 with contrast 5.67, where the middle gray pixel is double-counted into both clusters, understating the true extreme contrast of 21.0. The bug only manifests for odd pixel counts where round() rounds n/2 up; even counts produce exactly disjoint slices, which is why the two existing percentile=0.5 tests (both 4-pixel/even, lines 24 and 43) never caught it. Newness: pixel_contrast.py has a single commit (a79b768 feat: estimate screenshot pixel contrast); the overlap logic was never modified by round-1 fix commits (osc/safe_io, Otsu thresholding, cross-terminal OSC detection — all unrelated), and no test asserts cluster disjointness. The proposed fix (cap sample_size at len(pixels)//2) is correct. Severity LOW is justified: requires odd pixel count plus percentile near 0.5, while the default is 0.10 and real screenshots have many pixels where one overlapping pixel is negligible. It is nonetheless a genuine, API-reachable logic defect.
